macro_segments.title: Introduction to Bava Metzia: Structure, Themes, and the Two-Person Garment Case

micro_segments.title: Overview of Bava Metzia as the middle section of Nezikin with diverse topics
The classic case of two people holding a garment: equal claims and contested ownership
Why the oath must not undermine the claimant's own position; the principle of not suborning perjury
The function of the oath: getting liars to admit or stopping rationalizers from proceeding
The principle חשוד אממונא לא חשוד אשבועתא: suspicion of theft vs. suspicion of false swearing
How rationalizing differs from knowing theft; why oaths work as a deterrent to rationalization

macro_segments.title: Variant Claims and the Doctrine of Possession (Muchzak)

micro_segments.title: Case where one claims all and the other claims half; division into contested and conceded portions
Tosafot's question about migo: why the half-claimant cannot use migo to obtain the full half
The concept of muchzak (presumptive possession) and how claims affect default ownership status
The animal case: physical possession on an animal as equivalent to holding a garment
When agreement or witnesses eliminate the need for oath; the role of evidence in settlement

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Question: Why Two Cases Rather Than One, and the Distinction Between Finding and Purchase

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's opening question: why state both 'I found it' and 'it's all mine' rather than once?
Clarification that 'I found it' means physical possession, not mere sighting; עד שתא Ravina's interpretation
The distinction between מציאה (lost object) claims and מקח וממכר (purchase) claims; Rav Pappa's answer
Why מציאה cases allow rationalization: the found object was not desired beforehand
Why מקח וממכר cases also allow rationalization: money reimbursement softens the taking
The merchant case: both parties paid money, so the one who loses will be reimbursed by the merchant

macro_segments.title: Challenges to the Oath Requirement: Ben Nanas and Sumchus

micro_segments.title: Ben Nanas's objection: how can we allow שבועת שוא (false oaths) on both sides of a definite dispute?
The Gemara's response: the oath formulation allows for the possibility that both parties own half
Sumchus's position: when there is ספק (doubt), split without oath; applied to the calf-birth case
Why the calf case differs: no definite claim (שמא vs. ברי) means no effective oath can be formulated
The concept of דררא דממונא (objective stake): when the doubt arises from facts, not from claims alone
Sumchus's exception: he requires ברי claims to invoke oath; with objective doubt (שמא), no oath needed

macro_segments.title: The Kal Vachomer Argument: Why Sumchus Should Not Require Oath in the Two-Garment Case

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's counter-challenge: if Sumchus permits splitting without oath when taking from a חזקה, why not here?
In the calf case, the psak is admittedly not כפי האמת (according to truth), yet Sumchus permits it without oath
Here in the two-garment case, the psak of splitting could be according to truth (both own half), so oath should be less necessary
Unresolved tension: why Sumchus might still agree to oath in the garment case despite the kal vachomer

topical_tags.term: חשוד אממונא לא חשוד אשבועתא (suspicion of theft vs. suspicion of false oaths)
Ben Nanas (Tannaic disputant opposing oaths in definite disputes)
Sumchus (Tannaic disputant: split without oath when doubt exists)
יחלוקו (they split; division of contested property)
המוציא מחברו עליו הראיה (one who takes from another bears burden of proof)
כפי האמת (according to truth; truthfulness of a psak)
Ravina (Amoraic sage: finding a lost object means coming into possession)
Rav Pappa (Amoraic sage: distinguishes metzia from mekach umemkar cases)
